title: Testing the Compline Book Theme
content:
#+TITLE: Testing the Compline Book Theme
#+AUTHOR: Your Name


* Introduction
This is a test document to verify that the CSS layout is working correctly. If the CSS is loaded, you should see "Chapter 1" to the left of this heading.

** The Philosophy
The goal is to combine the aesthetic of a physical book with the dark, muted colors of the *Compline* Emacs theme.

* Technical Setup
This section tests how code blocks and sub-sections appear.

** Emacs Lisp Code
Here is a snippet of the configuration used to build this site. It should appear inside a dark box with a dotted left border.

#+begin_src elisp
(defun my/org-publish-success ()
  (message "The book theme has been applied!"))
#+end_src

** Styling Details
- *Bold text* for emphasis.
- /Italic text/ for definitions.
- ~Monospace~ for inline variables.

* Advanced Features
This section tests tables and folding details.

** Data Representation
| Feature          | Status    | Color Code |
|------------------+-----------+------------|
| Background       | Working   | #1a1d21    |
| Header Accent    | Testing   | #4d6b6b    |
| Link Visibility  | Pending   | #5a6b7a    |

** Hidden Implementation
#+begin_details
#+begin_summary
Technical Metadata
#+end_summary
This content is tucked away inside a details tag. It uses the `details` and `summary` CSS selectors we defined to keep the "book" look clean.
#+end_details

* Conclusion
If the text is readable and the margins are wide (centered), the theme is successful.

:END:
#+title: Scripting

* Main Goal
Learn Python + Bash + Go as tools while understanding:

- where memory lives
- what’s on the stack vs heap
- what’s contiguous vs scattered
- how OS + runtime mediate everything

title: rosetta-stone
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: 4447af37-b7e6-49ba-929b-82ee0e9e093e
:CREATED: [2026-04-16 Thu 12:44]
:END:
#+title: rosetta-stone

** Variables & Typing
How we declare, store, and modify data in memory, and how the language tracks the "type" of that data.

**** Python Implementation
Python uses dynamic typing. The interpreter figures out the type at runtime based on the value you assign.

#+begin_src python
# Declaration and assignment
name = "Elliot"
age = 25

# Re-assignment (can even change types)
age = "Twenty  Five" 

# Constants (No strict enforcement, just convention)
MAX_CONNECTIONS = 100
#+end_src

**** Bash Implementation
Bash treats almost everything as a string unless told otherwise. It is highly dependent on environment variables and exact syntax.

#+begin_src sh
# Declaration (NO spaces around the equals sign!)
name="Elliot"
age=55

# Re-assignment
age=26

# Constants
readonly MAX_CONNECTIONS=100

# Usage (Requires the $ prefix to read the value)
echo "My name is $name"
#+end_src

**** Go Implementation
Go is statically typed. The compiler must know the type of every variable before the program runs.

#+begin_src go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    // Standard declaration with explicit type
    var name string = "Elliot"
    
    // Short declaration (Type is inferred at compile time)
    age := 25 

    // Re-assignment (Must use = not :=, and must match original type)
    age = 26 

    // Constants
    const maxConnections = 100

    fmt.Println(name, age)
}
#+end_src

**** Exceptions & Paradigm Shifts
- In Bash, =name = "Elliot"= will throw an error because Bash thinks =name= is a command and === is the first argument.
- In Python and Bash, you can declare a variable and never use it. If you do this in Go, *the program will not compile*. Go enforces clean code at the compiler level.
- In Go, =:== declares a /new/ variable and assigns it. === only assigns a value to an /already existing/ variable. Using =:== on a variable that already exists will throw an error.


** Control Flow (If / Else)
Evaluating a boolean condition (True/False) to branch the execution path of the program.

**** Python Implementation
Python relies entirely on colons and strict whitespace indentation to define the block of code.

#+begin_src python
status_code = 404

if status_code == 200:
    print("Success")
elif status_code == 404:
    print("Not Found")
else:
    print("Unknown Error")
#+end_src

**** Bash Implementation
Bash uses bracket syntax for testing conditions. It requires explicit keywords (=then=, =elif=, =else=, and =fi=) to open and close blocks.

#+begin_src sh
status_code=404

# Spaces INSIDE the brackets are strictly required
if [ "$status_code" -eq 200 ]; then
    echo "Success"
elif [ "$status_code" -eq 404 ]; then
    echo "Not Found"
else
    echo "Unknown Error"
fi
#+end_src

**** Go Implementation
Go drops the parentheses around the condition (unlike C/C++) but enforces strict curly brace placement.

#+begin_src go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    statusCode := 404

    if statusCode == 200 {
        fmt.Println("Success")
    } else if statusCode == 404 {
        fmt.Println("Not Found")
    } else {
        fmt.Println("Unknown Error")
    }
}
#+end_src

**** Exceptions & Paradigm Shifts
- *Go's Initializer Statement:* This is a massive paradigm shift from Python. Go allows you to declare a variable /inside/ the =if= statement itself. The variable is only available inside that specific =if/else= block. This is how Go handles almost all error checking:
  #+begin_src go
  // Calls a function, assigns the result to err, then immediately checks if err is not nil
  if err := processData(); err != nil {
      fmt.Println("Failed:", err)
      // handle error
  }
  // 'err' no longer exists down here
  #+end_src

- *Go's Strict Braces:* In Go, the opening curly brace ={= *must* be on the same line as the =if= or =else= keyword. If you press Enter and put the ={= on the next line, the Go compiler will fail.
- *Bash String vs. Integer Comparisons:* Python and Go use ==== for both strings and numbers. Bash requires different operators. Use =-eq=, =-lt=, =-gt= for numbers, but use === or =!== for strings. Example: =if [ "$name" = "Elliot" ]; then=.

title: Org Agenda
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID:       c5b00cea-c246-44c3-a084-fba3f8f87c1e
:CREATED: [2026-01-20 Tue 19:29]
:END:
#+title: Org Agenda
#+author: Hann Harri 

* Basic Usage

** Open the agenda
Press =SPC o a= (or =C-c a=). This opens the agenda dispatcher:

- =a= :: Agenda (week view with scheduled items)
- =t= :: Todo list (all open TODOs)
- =d= :: Dashboard (custom view)
  - Shows this week
  - Next tasks
  - Waiting items

* Key Workflow Commands

** Creating and scheduling tasks

1. Create a task in =~/org/todo.org=:
   #+begin_src org
   * TODO Write report
   #+end_src

2. Schedule the task  
   Place the cursor on the task headline and press =C-c C-s=.  
   Pick a date to make it appear in the agenda.

3. Set a deadline (optional)  
   Press =C-c C-d=.  
   - *Scheduled* = when you plan to work on it  
   - *Deadline* = must be done *by* this date

4. Mark task as done  
   Press =C-c C-t= and select =DONE=.  
   Completion time is automatically logged.

* Configuration Improvements

The enhanced configuration provides:

- *Custom agenda views*  
  =org-agenda-custom-commands=
  - =d= :: Dashboard
  - =t= :: Today
  - =n= :: Next
  - =w= :: Waiting

- *Better logging*  
  =org-log-done= timestamps task completion in a hidden drawer

- *Smart skipping*  
  Completed tasks do not clutter the agenda

- *Deadline warnings*  
  Deadlines are flagged 7 days in advance

* Suggested File Structure

#+begin_example
~/org/inbox.org  - Quick capture dump
~/org/todo.org   - Main task list (organized by project)
~/org/notes.org  - Reference / knowledge notes
#+end_example

Use a GTD-style workflow:
- Capture everything quickly into =inbox.org=
- Periodically process and move items into =todo.org=

* Pro Tips

- Use *tags* like =:work:=, =:home:=, =:urgent:=  
  Filter with:
  =C-c a m work=

- Keep =~/org/roam= separate for your knowledge base

- Use a =WAIT= TODO state for tasks blocked by others

- Check your dashboard daily:
  =SPC o a= then =d=

* Practice

Create a few test tasks in =~/org/todo.org= and schedule them.  
The agenda workflow will feel natural very quickly.

title: into-sql
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: ca3e4347-82cf-4afc-87b7-3a1309760005
:CREATED: [2026-05-02 Sat 19:42]
:END:
#+title: into-sql

* Database file

A database file (like an =.mdf= file in SQL Server) is just a massive continuous file on your hard drive. To manage memory efficiently, the database chops this file into *8 Kilobyte (8192 bytes)* blocks. This is the smallest unit of disk I/O. Every time the database reads or writes, it moves an entire 8KB Page into RAM.

Pages are blocks (8Kb) inside larger database files, not individual files.

When querying the database does not load the whole file into RAM unless needed, instead it reads only the required Pages from disk and loads them into RAM while also caching frequently used Pages. 

This is the general layout of a database files (conceptual) 

#+begin_src text
  Database System
│
├── Logical Layer
│   ├── Tables
│   ├── Views
│   ├── Indexes
│   └── Schemas
│
└── Physical Layer
    ├── Data Files
    │    └── Contain many 8KB pages
    │
    ├── Index Files
    │    └── Contain many index pages
    │
    ├── Transaction Logs (WAL / Redo)
    │
    ├── Metadata/System Catalog
    │
    └── Buffer Pool (RAM cache)
#+end_src


Where one large file contains many pages:

#+begin_src text
  employees_table.dat
│
├── Page 0 (8KB)
├── Page 1 (8KB)
├── Page 2 (8KB)
├── Page 3 (8KB)
└── ...
#+end_src

This is just one file internally divided into Pages.

#+begin_note
*Logical vs Physical Layer*
Logical Layer stores the metadata and schema definition. When you =CREATE= a table all the information about the table like the table name, columns, data types, constraints, keys, index type, and permissions are stored in the logical layer

The Physical Layer hold the actual data. So all the values you =INSERT= goes into the table Pages.

Conceptually, a table is not stored as Pages but it's rows are stored in Pages.
#+end_note



#+begin_note
*Data Modification*

If a row is updated

The Page containing that row data is loaded into RAM, updated and the change is written to WAL. It is later flushed back to disk
#+end_note

There are two main types of pages:

- *Data Page:* Stores your actual table rows.  
- *Index Page:* Stores traffic signs (Keys and memory pointers) telling the database where to find the Data Pages.
    

* Data Page

When you insert a row, the database writes it into a Data Page. The page fills up from the top down, while an "Offset Array" (a map of where each row starts) fills up from the bottom up.



#+begin_src text
+----------------------------------------------------+
|                   PAGE HEADER                      |
| (96 Bytes) Page ID, Object ID, Free Space count    |
+----------------------------------------------------+
| Row 1: [ID: 1, Name: "Alice", Role: "Engineer"]    |
|----------------------------------------------------|
| Row 2: [ID: 2, Name: "Bob", Role: "Sales"]         |
|----------------------------------------------------|
| Row 3: [ID: 3, Name: "Charlie", Role: "Admin"]     |
|----------------------------------------------------|
|                                                    |
|                   FREE SPACE                       |
|          (Rows grow down, offsets grow up)         |
|                                                    |
|----------------------------------------------------|
| Offset 3 (Points to Row 3 byte address)            |
|----------------------------------------------------|
| Offset 2 (Points to Row 2 byte address)            |
|----------------------------------------------------|
| Offset 1 (Points to Row 1 byte address)            |
+----------------------------------------------------+
#+end_src

Note: If you try to insert a massive string that exceeds 8KB, the database has to split it across multiple pages, which kills performance. This is why strict schema design matters.


* Heap Data Structure

If you create a table without a Primary Key or a Clustered Index, you create a *Heap*.

A Heap is literally just an unordered pile of Data Pages.

- *Write Performance (Fast):* When you =INSERT=, the database just finds the last page and blindly appends the data. It doesn't waste CPU sorting anything.
    
- *Read Performance (Terrible):* If you search for =ID = 50=, the database has no idea where it is. It must perform a Table Scan, reading every single 8KB Data Page from the disk into RAM until it finds the row.
    

* The Clustered Index (B-Tree)

A Clustered Index fundamentally physically rearranges how your Data Pages sit on the hard drive. **The table data itself becomes the leaf nodes of a B-Tree.** You can only have one Clustered Index per table because the data can only be sorted physically in one way (usually by the Primary Key).

- **Read Performance (Fast):** It traverses the tree logarithmically.
    
- **Write Performance (Slower):** If you insert `ID = 25` between `24` and `26`, and that Data Page is full, the database has to perform a "Page Split"—creating a new page, moving half the rows over, and rewriting the Index Pages. This is expensive.
    

**Visualizing the Clustered Index (B-Tree)**

Imagine searching for `ID = 55`. The database reads the Root Node, follows the pointer to the correct Intermediate Node, and lands directly on the Data Page.


                          [ ROOT INDEX PAGE   ]
                          | ID Range: 1 - 100 |
                          +---------+---------+
                                    |
                  +-----------------+-----------------+
                  |                                   |
                  V                                   V
        [ INTERMEDIATE PAGE ]               [ INTERMEDIATE PAGE ]
        | ID Range: 1 - 50  |               | ID Range: 51 - 100|
        +----+---------+----+               +----+---------+----+
             |         |                         |         |
      +------+         +------+           +------+         +------+
      |                       |           |                       |
      V                       V           V                       V
[ DATA PAGE 1 ]         [ DATA PAGE 2]  [ DATA PAGE 3 ]         [ DATA PAGE 4 ]
| ROW: ID 1   |         | ROW: ID 26 |  | ROW: ID 51  |         | ROW: ID 76  |
| ROW: ID 2   |         | ROW: ID 27 |  | ROW: ID 52  |         | ROW: ID 77  |
| ...         |         | ...        |  | ...         |         | ...         |
| ROW: ID 25  |         | ROW: ID 50 |  | ROW: ID 75  |         | ROW: ID 100 |
+-------------+         +------------+  +-------------+         +-------------+
                 ^^ THESE ARE YOUR ACTUAL TABLE ROWS ^^


* The Non-Clustered Index (Lookup Table)

What if your data is physically clustered by `ID`, but you frequently search by `Email`? You create a **Non-Clustered Index**.

A Non-Clustered Index is a _completely separate B-Tree_. The key difference: **The leaf nodes are NOT Data Pages. The leaf nodes are Index Pages that just hold memory pointers back to the Clustered Index.**

- **Read Performance (Fast for lookups, slow for full rows):** It finds the pointer instantly, but to get the rest of the row data, it has to do a "Key Lookup" back to the Clustered Index.
    
- **Write Performance (Faster than Clustered, but adds overhead):** It doesn't physically move your massive table data, but every time you `INSERT` a row, the database must update the main table _and_ update this secondary tree.
    

**Visualizing the Non-Clustered Index (on "Name")**

If you run `SELECT * FROM table WHERE Name = 'Bob';`


                          [ ROOT INDEX PAGE ]
                          | Names: A - Z    |
                          +---------+---------+
                                    |
                  +-----------------+-----------------+
                  |                                   |
                  V                                   V
        [ INTERMEDIATE PAGE ]               [ INTERMEDIATE PAGE ]
        | Names: A - M      |               | Names: N - Z      |
        +----+---------+----+               +----+---------+----+
             |                                   |
             V                                   V
     [ LEAF INDEX PAGE ]                 [ LEAF INDEX PAGE ]
     | "Alice" -> Point to ID 1 |        | "Nancy" -> Point to ID 8 |
     | "Bob"   -> Point to ID 2 |        | "Zack"  -> Point to ID 9 |
     +--------------------------+        +--------------------------+
                  |
                  | (Database follows pointer to ID 2)
                  V
     [ ACTUAL CLUSTERED DATA PAGE ]
     | ROW: ID 2, "Bob", "Sales"  |
     +----------------------------+

title: Into-Git
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: 1b871274-d1ae-4af5-ac03-a71aaf75b7ee
:CREATED: [2026-03-18 Wed 17:28]
:END:
#+title: Into-Git

** Git

Local Git Directory Layout

When you run git init in a folder:

A hidden .git/ directory is created.
It contains all the version history, branches, tags, and staging data.
If .git/ is deleted, the folder is no longer a Git repo.

** Status

A file can be in one of several states in a Git repository. Here are a few important ones:

untracked: Not being tracked by Git
staged: Marked for inclusion in the next commit
committed: Saved to the repository's history

The git status command shows you the current state of your repo. It will tell you which files are untracked, staged, and committed.

** Staging

The contents.md file has been created, but as we saw, it's untracked. We need to stage it (add it to the "index") with the git add command before committing it later.

Without staging, no files are included in the commit—only the files you explicitly git add will be committed.

Here's the command:

~git add <path-to-file | pattern>~

** Commit

After staging a file, we can commit it.

A commit is a snapshot of the repository at a given point in time. It's a way to save the state of the repository, and it's how Git keeps track of changes to the project. A commit comes with a message that describes the changes made in the commit.

Here's how to commit all of your staged files:

~git commit -m "your message here"~

*** What happens when you commit?
Git takes a snapshot of the file,
Stores it as a blob object,
Records a tree object representing directory structure,
Creates a commit object pointing to the tree + metadata (author, timestamp, message).


** Git Log

A Git repo is a (potentially very long) list of commits, where each commit represents the full state of the repository at a given point in time.

The git log command shows a history of the commits in a repository. This is what makes Git a version control system. You can see:

    Who made a commit
    When the commit was made
    What was changed

~git --no-pager log -n 10~
or
~git --no-pager log -n 10 --oneline --parents --graph~ 
=git --no-pager log --oneline --graph --decorate --all=


* Inode busting???

A Commit Hash

Each commit has a unique identifier called a "commit hash". This is a long string of characters that uniquely identifies the commit. Here's an example of mine:

~5ba786fcc93e8092831c01e71444b9baa2228a4f~

For convenience, you can refer to any commit or change within Git by using the first 7 characters of its hash. For mine, that's 5ba786f.

You may have noticed that even though we (you and I) both have the same content in our repositories, we have different commit hashes. While commit hashes are derived from their content changes, there's also some other stuff that affects the end hash. For example:

    The commit message
    The author's name and email
    The date and time
    Parent (previous) commit hashes

All this to say that hashes are effectively unique in practice. While SHA-1 collisions are possible under contrived conditions, you won't accidentally create two different commits with the same hash, and because they're generated automatically for you, you don't need to worry too much about what goes into them right now.
Note: Hash = SHA

Git uses a cryptographic hash function called SHA-1 to generate commit hashes. We won't go into the details of how SHA-1 works in this course, but it's important to know because you might also hear commit hashes referred to as "SHAs".

** Notes
You cab see Tree as a directory and blob as a file
git cat-file -p <hash>

** Commit Quality
Commits should contain changes related to a **single topic** to maintain clarity and ease of understanding for both current and future collaborators.
- **Selective Staging:** The Git staging area enables adding **specific files or even parts of files** (`git add -p`) to ensure commits stay focused.
- **Commit Message Best Practices:**
  - Keep the **subject line concise**, ideally under 80 characters.
  - Use the commit message body to answer:
    - *What changed?*
    - *Why was the change made?*
    - *Are there any notable implications or points to watch out for?*
  - A clear message aids code review and future maintenance.:

#### Branching Strategies
- Git provides tools but **does not enforce branching conventions**; teams must define and document their own strategies.
- Branch conventions help avoid conflicts, improve project organization, and assist onboarding.
- Two extreme workflow models:
  - **Always Be Integrating (ABI):** A single branch where all commits are integrated frequently. Requires small commits and strong testing/QA to maintain quality.
  - **Multiple Branch Types:** Separate branches for features, releases, experiments, and different deployment states (e.g., develop, staging, production). More complex but flexible.
- **Long-running branches:** Exist throughout the project lifecycle (e.g., `main`, `develop`, `staging`). Direct commits are usually avoided; changes come through merges or rebases.
- **Short-lived branches:** Created for specific tasks like features, bug fixes, or experiments; deleted after integration.

#### Popular Branching Workflows
| Workflow    | Description                                                                                           | Branch Types                         |
|-------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| GitHub Flow | Simple, single long-running branch (`main`), all work done in short-lived branches for features or fixes. | 1 long-running (`main`), many short-lived |
| Git Flow    | Structured with `main`, `develop`, release, and feature branches. Supports release management and QA. | Multiple long-running and short-lived branches |

- Tools like Tower GUI can simplify following branching workflows by automating common tasks.

#### Pull Requests (PRs)
- PRs are **not a core Git feature** but provided by hosting platforms (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket).
- Purpose:
  - Facilitate **code review and collaboration** by inviting feedback before merging.
  - Enable contributions to repositories where you have no direct write access (via forks).
- Workflow example:
  - Fork a repository.
  - Create a feature branch.
  - Make and commit changes.
  - Push branch to your fork.
  - Open a PR to propose merging changes back to the original repository.
- PR interfaces vary by platform but share the same principles.

#### Merge Conflicts
- Occur during integration actions like merge, rebase, cherry-pick, pull, or stash application.
- Typical cause: **Contradictory changes**, e.g., same line altered differently or file modified in one branch and deleted in another.
- Git detects conflicts and **halts the operation**, requiring manual resolution.
- Conflict markers in files:
  - `<<<<<<< HEAD` - current branch changes
  - `=======` - separator
  - `>>>>>>> other-branch` - incoming branch changes
- Resolution:
  - Manually edit files to choose or combine changes.
  - Use GUI tools (e.g., Tower) or dedicated merge tools for visualization and easier conflict resolution.
- You can **abort merge or rebase conflicts** anytime using commands like `git merge --abort` or `git rebase --abort`.
- After resolving conflicts, commit the changes to complete the integration.

#### Integrating Branches: Merge vs. Rebase
| Feature          | Merge                                    | Rebase                                  |
|------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Resulting History| Creates a **merge commit** connecting branches, retaining branch structure | Rewrites commit history to create a **linear history** without merge commits |
| Use Case         | Common for shared branches, preserves context of branch topology | Ideal for cleaning local commit history before integration |
| Risks            | Can clutter history with merge commits  | Rewriting history can cause issues if commits are already pushed/shared |
| Command          | `git merge <branch>`                     | `git rebase <branch>`                   |

- **Important rule:** Avoid rebasing commits that have been pushed to shared repositories to prevent conflicts for collaborators.

---

### Timeline Table of Topics Covered

| Timestamp Range      | Topic                              | Key Points                                                                                  |
|----------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 00:00:00 – 00:03:00  | Introduction & Commit Basics      | Importance of focused commits, selective staging (`git add -p`)                             |
| 00:06:00 – 00:08:10  | Commit message best practices     | Concise subject, detailed body explaining what, why, and special notes                      |
| 00:08:15 – 00:12:17  | Branching strategies overview     | Team conventions, long-running vs. short-lived branches                                    |
| 00:12:20 – 00:16:37  | Popular branching workflows       | GitHub Flow (simple), Git Flow (structured with develop and release branches)              |
| 00:17:22 – 00:24:43  | Pull requests & forks             | Purpose, workflow, example using GitHub and Ruby on Rails repository                        |
| 00:24:44 – 00:33:49  | Merge conflicts & resolution      | Causes, detection, manual and GUI-based resolution, aborting conflicts                      |
| 00:33:55 – 00:40:38  | Merge vs. rebase                  | How merge works (merge commits), how rebase rewrites history, when to use each             |

---

### Core Concepts Explained

- **Git staging area:** Buffer zone to choose exactly which changes to include in the next commit.
- **Long-running branches:** Persistent branches representing main development or deployment stages.
- **Short-lived branches:** Temporary branches for isolated work, deleted after integration.
- **Pull requests:** Mechanism for code review and collaboration on Git hosting platforms.
- **Merge conflict:** Occurs when two branches have incompatible changes that Git cannot automatically reconcile.
- **Merge commit:** A commit automatically created by Git to join two divergent branches.
- **Rebase:** Reapplies commits on top of another branch to create a linear history, rewriting commit hashes.

title: http-intro
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: d44350f1-5d07-407e-ab5a-8ae406e4530b
:CREATED: [2026-04-14 Tue 10:27]
:END:
#+title: http-intro

** What is HTTP?
Hyper Text Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is like a conversation between a client and a server, where every interaction is a request and a respone. HTTP is stateless that means it doesn't remember anything about previous transactions (a request and a response). When a client sends a request =GET /profile=  the server responds with the header and if the client requests =GET /order= the server doesn't know they are the same clients. So how does a server know it's the same person who islogged in? This is usually handled through tokens and cookies. 


** Anatomy of an HTTP Request

#+begin_src text
GET /users/123 HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
User-Agent: curl/8.0
Authorization: Bearer abc123
#+end_src

** HTTPS?

Hyper Text Transfer Protocol Secure
Data is encrypted by SSL/TLS
Secure Socket Layer
Transport Security Layer

** HTTP Methods
*** GET
*** POST
*** PUT
*** DELETE

-  path

  General, Response, request

title: Go: Struct, Method, Interface
go-rust
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: 5acf2f26-498f-451e-b67f-d59469684f43
:CREATED: [2026-04-17 Fri 11:25]
:END:
#+title: go-rust

** Word Frequency Counter

*Go*
#+begin_src go
func wordFreq(s string) map[string]int {
	freq := make(map[string]int)
	words := strings.Split(s, " ")

	for _, word := range words {
		freq[word]++
	}
	return freq
}
#+end_src

*Rust*
#+begin_src rust
fn word_freq(s: &str) -> HashMap<String, i32> {
    let mut freq = HashMap::new();

    for word in s.split_whitespace() {
        let count = freq.entry(word.to_string()).or_insert(0);
        *count += 1;
    }
    freq
}
#+end_src

A string ~s~ is defined as the input for the function. Go uses a map ~map[string]int~ for paired values, like dictionary in python. Rust uses HashMap that takes a ~String~ and a 32-bit integer. Go and Rust requires that you define the output type in the function. 
This is how it's defined in python but it's not necessary (dynamic typing).
  
#+begin_src python 
def wordFreq(s: str) -> dict[str, int]:
#+end_src 


~entry(word.to_string())~: checks if the word exists in the HashMap and, if it does, returns a mutable reference to the value. If it doesn’t exist, it inserts the default value (0 in this case). The ~or_insert~ method is used to either get the current value or insert the default one.

The ~entry()~ API is not strictly necessary in Rust for simple cases. You could also do:
~freq[word.to_string()] += 1;~

This would also work, but it could panic if the word doesn’t exist in the map yet, whereas the ~entry()~ approach prevents that and gives more control (especially when inserting default values).

~to_string()~ converts the ~&str~ (borrowed string slice) into an owned ~String~. This is needed because HashMap in Rust uses ~String~ as the key type, and ~&str~ is not compatible directly as a key in the map.


#+begin_note
*Why use &str instead of String?*

~&str~ is a /string slice/ in Rust. It’s a reference to a string, but it does not own the string data. It’s a borrowed view into the string and ~s~ is just the name of the parameter that represents this string slice.
A ~String~ is owned, meaning it has its own memory allocation while a ~&str~ is a borrowed reference
When you use ~&str~, you're passing around references to the data, not owning it. This can lead to better performance and memory management, as Rust doesn't need to copy or allocate extra memory.
~String~ is for when you need to own the string data, especially if you're going to mutate or store it elsewhere.

~let s = "hello";~   This is a &str, borrowed
~let owned_s = String::from("hello");~  This is an owned String
#+end_note

title: Go: Pointers and State Mutation
GO Basics
git-internals
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: 4150f1d1-be4f-43b3-808e-2dded5332a59
:CREATED: [2026-04-18 Sat 23:51]
:END:
#+title: git-internals

** What happens inside .git/ 

Let's explore a ~.git/~ directory

#+begin_src bash
ls -a .git
#+end_src

| Directory/File | Purpose                                                  |
|----------------+----------------------------------------------------------|
| =objects/=     | Stores all the Git objects (commits, blobs, trees, tags) |
| =refs/=        | Stores references to commits (like =main=)               |
| =HEAD=         | Points to the current branch                             |
| =config=       | Local repo config                                        |
| =hooks/=       | Scripts that run on certain Git events                   |

** So what are git objects?

When you work with Git, every piece of your project's history is stored as something called a "Git object". To understand this better, imagine Git as a smart photo archive for your code. Every time you take a snapshot of your project using git commit, it stores the content of your files as individual photos (called blobs), records how your folders are organized (using trees), and writes a detailed caption describing when the snapshot was taken, who took it, and what changed (stored as commits). Each of these components gets a unique fingerprint so Git can find and reference them later.

Git runs every piece of content through a SHA-1 hash function, which produces a 40-character string that serves as that object's permanent ID. This hash is like a fingerprint where no two different pieces of content will ever produce the same hash, and the same content will always produce the exact same hash.

Wait, it doesn't stop there. Git doesn't just hash your raw file content. Before creating the hash, Git adds a small header that describes what type of object it is and how big the content is. For example, if you have a file containing "hello friend", Git first creates a string that looks like ~blob 12\0hello friend~ (where =blob= indicates the type, =12= is the number of characters, =\0= is a null separator, and then comes your actual content). Only then does it calculate the SHA-1 hash.


***** Git's Content-Addressed Storage System
Git operates as a content-addressed storage system. This means that instead of storing files by their names or locations, Git stores everything based on its content. The SHA-1 hash of each object serves as both its unique identifier and its storage address within the =.git/objects/= directory.

This means identical content is never stored twice. Git can quickly determine whether it already has a particular piece of content just by calculating its hash. And, if any stored content gets corrupted, its hash won't match anymore, and Git will know something went wrong.


***** How Git Avoids Duplication
Let's walk through a practical example to see how Git's deduplication works. Suppose you create a file called greeting.txt with the content "hello friend" and commit it to your repository. Git calculates the hash of this content and stores it as a blob object.

Later, you decide to create another file called message.txt with exactly the same content: "hello friend." When you add this new file to Git, something interesting happens. Git calculates the hash of the new file's content and discovers it's identical to the hash of the blob it created earlier. Instead of creating a new blob object, Git simply references the existing one.

From Git's perspective, both files point to the same blob object in its object database. The only thing that changes is the tree objects (which we'll explore later) that record the different filenames and their locations in your project structure. This means your Git repository stores the content "hello friend" only once, even though it appears in multiple files across potentially many commits.

#+begin_src bash
                ┌────────────────────┐
                │     Commit         │
                │--------------------│
                │ Author, Timestamp  │
                │ Message            │
                │ Points to → Tree   │────┐
                └────────────────────┘    │
                                          ▼
                                 ┌────────────────────┐
                                 │       Tree         │
                                 │--------------------│
                                 │ "hello.txt" → Blob │◄─┐
                                 │ "subdir/"   → Tree │  │
                                 └────────────────────┘  │
                                                         ▼
                                        ┌────────────────────┐
                                        │       Blob         │
                                        │--------------------│
                                        │ File content only  │
                                        │ "hello friend"     │
                                        └────────────────────┘
#+end_src

| Type     | What it Represents | Analogy                                   |
|----------+--------------------+-------------------------------------------|
| =blob=   | File content       | The actual photo of a file                |
| =tree=   | Directory snapshot | A folder, listing file names + blobs      |
| =commit= | A snapshot in time | A pointer to the root tree + metadata     |
| =tag=    | Named pointer      | A post-it stuck on a commit saying “v1.0” |

So Git isn’t saving diffs. It saves the full structure of the project at each commit (with hashing and compression).

** So what is git index?

Git index is like the shopping cart of your changes. 
 You can:
- change your shopping list (working dir)
- add items to your cart (index via =git add=)
- remove items (=git restore --staged=)
- checkout (=git commit=) to get your final receipt (commit object)


When you =git add= a file, Git:

1. Hash the content to get the blob hash
2. Store that blob in =.git/objects/= if it’s new
3. Add an entry to .git/index:
   - File path: file.txt
   - Blob SHA: (e.g., a8b1f8...)
   - Metadata: permissions, size, timestamp
     

Everything staged is stored in one binary file =.git/index=
It's structured like a table/list, with one entry per file, containing path, mode (permissions), SHA-1 hash (of blob)
timestamps and size, stage (for merge cases)

#+begin_src text
.git/index
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   File Path   │  Mode  │ Blob SHA │  Stage │
├───────────────┼────────┼──────────┼────────┤
│ file.txt      │ 100644 │ a8b1f8…  │    0   │
└───────────────┴────────┴──────────┴────────┘
#+end_src

#+begin_quote
The =Stage= is usually 0 (unless in a merge).
#+end_quote


git ls-files --stage
git rev-list --all --objects
git cat-file -t e7fbc32     # Shows type (commit, tree, blob)
git cat-file -p e7fbc32     # Pretty-print the object

title: git-basics
content:
:PROPERTIES:
:ID: 3d5dfc3b-3a8a-4646-844a-8aca994a7f64
:CREATED: [2026-04-23 Thu 13:37]
:END:
#+title: git-basics

* Git Config

Git stores author information so that when you're making a commit it can track who made the change. 

Let's take the command apart:

git config: The command to interact with your Git configuration.
set: The subcommand to set a value – i.e., to add it if it doesn't already exist, or update it if it does.
--global: Flag stating you want this configuration to be stored globally in your ~/.gitconfig. The opposite is --local, which stores the configuration in the current repository only.
user: The section.
name: The key within the section.
"ThePrimeagen": The value you want to set for the key.
    
Git has a command to view the contents of your config:

git config list --local

You can also just view the contents of your local config file directly:

cat .git/config

** System, Global, local, worktree
There are several locations where Git can be configured. From more general to more specific, they are:

    system: /etc/gitconfig, a file that configures Git for all users on the system
    global: ~/.gitconfig, a file that configures Git for all projects of a user
    local: .git/config, a file that configures Git for a specific project
    worktree: .git/config.worktree, a file that configures Git for part of a project

In my experience, 90% of the time you will be using --global to set things like your username and email. The other 9% of the time you will be using --local to set project-specific configurations. The last 1% of the time you might need to futz with system and worktree configurations, but it's extremely rare.


** Get

We've used list to see all the configuration values, but the get subcommand is useful for getting a single value.

git config get <key>

Keys are in the format <section>.<keyname>.

** Unset
Unset

The unset subcommand is used to remove a configuration value. For example:

git config unset <key>


Only works with section and a key =<section>.<keyname>=

**  remove-section

=git config --remove-section <section>=


** Unset-all

=git config --unset-all webflyx.ceo=

Removes all


* Git branching

A Git branch allows you to keep track of different changes separately.

For example, let's say you have a big web project and you want to experiment with changing the color scheme. Instead of changing the entire project directly (as of right now, our master branch), you can create a new branch called color_scheme and work on that branch. When you're done, if you like the changes, you can merge the color_scheme branch back into the master branch to keep the changes. If you don't like the changes, you can simply delete the color_scheme branch and go back to the master branch.
Under the Hood

A branch is just a named pointer to a specific commit. When you create a branch, you are creating a new pointer to a specific commit. The commit that the branch points to is called the tip of the branch.
Because a branch is just a pointer to a commit, they're lightweight and "cheap" resource-wise to create. When you create 10 branches, you're not creating 10 copies of your project on your hard drive.

Check which branch you're currently on by running:

=git branch=

Rename a branch
=git branch -m oldname newname=

** New Branch

You should already be on the main branch: your "default" branch. You can always check with git branch.
Two Ways to Create a Branch

=git branch my_new_branch=

This creates a new branch called =my_new_branch=. The thing is, I rarely use this command because usually I want to create a branch and switch to it immediately. So I use this command instead:

=git switch -c my_new_branch=

=-c= is for create. Use without c to just switch.

The switch command allows you to switch branches. Including the -c flag tells Git to create a new branch and switch to it.

When you create a new branch, it uses the current commit you are on as the branch base. For example, if you're on your main branch with 3 commits, A, B, and C, and then you run =git switch -c my_new_branch=, your new branch will look like this:

Another way to switch

=git checkout prime=

** Merge

=git merge branch_to_merge=

View the commits and merges
 =git log --oneline --decorate --graph --parents=

*** Fastforwarsd merge
The branch you are merging onto if the tip of that branch is the merge base - fast forward branch
Basically there is no branch.

** Delete a branch

=git branch -d branch_name=



I need some notes of these
--oneline
--graph
--parents
--all
and other stuff that's very commonly used and would be useful

** Rebase
Moves the branch to the tip of the base of the main branch that the feature branch is based on 

Visualizing Rebase

Say we have this commit history:

#+begin_src bash
A - B - C    main
   \
    D - E    feature_branch
#+end_src

We're working on =feature_branch=, and want to bring in the changes our team added to main so we're not working with a stale branch. We could merge main into =feature_branch=, but that would create an additional merge commit. Rebase avoids a merge commit by replaying the commits from =feature_branch= on top of main. After a rebase, the history will look like this:

#+begin_src bash
A - B - C         main
         \
          D - E   feature_branch
#+end_src
